952	@array_function_dispatch(_nanmean_dispatcher)
953	def nanmean(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, keepdims=np._NoValue,
954	            *, where=np._NoValue):
955	    """
956	    Compute the arithmetic mean along the specified axis, ignoring NaNs.
957	
958	    Returns the average of the array elements.  The average is taken over
959	    the flattened array by default, otherwise over the specified axis.
960	    `float64` intermediate and return values are used for integer inputs.
961	
962	    For all-NaN slices, NaN is returned and a `RuntimeWarning` is raised.
963	
964	    Parameters
965	    ----------
966	    a : array_like
967	        Array containing numbers whose mean is desired. If `a` is not an
968	        array, a conversion is attempted.
969	    axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional
970	        Axis or axes along which the means are computed. The default is to compute
971	        the mean of the flattened array.
972	    dtype : data-type, optional
973	        Type to use in computing the mean.  For integer inputs, the default
974	        is `float64`; for inexact inputs, it is the same as the input
975	        dtype.
976	    out : ndarray, optional
977	        Alternate output array in which to place the result.  The default
978	        is ``None``; if provided, it must have the same shape as the
979	        expected output, but the type will be cast if necessary.
980	        See :ref:`ufuncs-output-type` for more details.
981	    keepdims : bool, optional
982	        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left
983	        in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,
984	        the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.
985	
986	        If the value is anything but the default, then
987	        `keepdims` will be passed through to the `mean` or `sum` methods
988	        of sub-classes of `ndarray`.  If the sub-classes methods
989	        does not implement `keepdims` any exceptions will be raised.
990	    where : array_like of bool, optional
991	        Elements to include in the mean. See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce` for details.
992	
993	        .. versionadded:: 1.22.0
994	
995	    Returns
996	    -------
997	    m : ndarray, see dtype parameter above
998	        If `out=None`, returns a new array containing the mean values,
999	        otherwise a reference to the output array is returned. Nan is
1000	        returned for slices that contain only NaNs.
1001	
1002	    See Also
1003	    --------
1004	    average : Weighted average
1005	    mean : Arithmetic mean taken while not ignoring NaNs
1006	    var, nanvar
1007	
1008	    Notes
1009	    -----
1010	    The arithmetic mean is the sum of the non-NaN elements along the axis
1011	    divided by the number of non-NaN elements.
1012	
1013	    Note that for floating-point input, the mean is computed using the same
1014	    precision the input has.  Depending on the input data, this can cause
1015	    the results to be inaccurate, especially for `float32`.  Specifying a
1016	    higher-precision accumulator using the `dtype` keyword can alleviate
1017	    this issue.
1018	
1019	    Examples
1020	    --------
1021	    >>> import numpy as np
1022	    >>> a = np.array([[1, np.nan], [3, 4]])
1023	    >>> np.nanmean(a)
1024	    2.6666666666666665
1025	    >>> np.nanmean(a, axis=0)
1026	    array([2.,  4.])
1027	    >>> np.nanmean(a, axis=1)
1028	    array([1.,  3.5]) # may vary
1029	
1030	    """
1031	    arr, mask = _replace_nan(a, 0)
1032	    if mask is None:
1033	        return np.mean(arr, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, keepdims=keepdims,
1034	                       where=where)
1035	
1036	    if dtype is not None:
1037	        dtype = np.dtype(dtype)
1038	    if dtype is not None and not issubclass(dtype.type, np.inexact):
1039	        raise TypeError("If a is inexact, then dtype must be inexact")
1040	    if out is not None and not issubclass(out.dtype.type, np.inexact):
1041	        raise TypeError("If a is inexact, then out must be inexact")
1042	
1043	    cnt = np.sum(~mask, axis=axis, dtype=np.intp, keepdims=keepdims,
1044	                 where=where)
1045	    tot = np.sum(arr, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, keepdims=keepdims,
1046	                 where=where)
1047	    avg = _divide_by_count(tot, cnt, out=out)
1048	
1049	    isbad = (cnt == 0)
1050	    if isbad.any():
1051	        warnings.warn("Mean of empty slice", RuntimeWarning, stacklevel=2)
1052	        # NaN is the only possible bad value, so no further
1053	        # action is needed to handle bad results.
1054	    return avg
1055

1057	def _nanmedian1d(arr1d, overwrite_input=False):
1058	    """
1059	    Private function for rank 1 arrays. Compute the median ignoring NaNs.
1060	    See nanmedian for parameter usage
1061	    """
1062	    arr1d_parsed, _, overwrite_input = _remove_nan_1d(
1063	        arr1d, overwrite_input=overwrite_input,
1064	    )
1065	
1066	    if arr1d_parsed.size == 0:
1067	        # Ensure that a nan-esque scalar of the appropriate type (and unit)
1068	        # is returned for `timedelta64` and `complexfloating`
1069	        return arr1d[-1]

1071	    return np.median(arr1d_parsed, overwrite_input=overwrite_input)

1074	def _nanmedian(a, axis=None, out=None, overwrite_input=False):
1075	    """
1076	    Private function that doesn't support extended axis or keepdims.
1077	    These methods are extended to this function using _ureduce
1078	    See nanmedian for parameter usage
1079	
1080	    """
1081	    if axis is None or a.ndim == 1:
1082	        part = a.ravel()
1083	        if out is None:
1084	            return _nanmedian1d(part, overwrite_input)
1085	        else:
1086	            out[...] = _nanmedian1d(part, overwrite_input)
1087	            return out
1088	    else:
1089	        # for small medians use sort + indexing which is still faster than
1090	        # apply_along_axis
1091	        # benchmarked with shuffled (50, 50, x) containing a few NaN
1092	        if a.shape[axis] < 600:
1093	            return _nanmedian_small(a, axis, out, overwrite_input)
1094	        result = np.apply_along_axis(_nanmedian1d, axis, a, overwrite_input)
1095	        if out is not None:
1096	            out[...] = result
1097	        return result
1098

1100	def _nanmedian_small(a, axis=None, out=None, overwrite_input=False):
1101	    """
1102	    sort + indexing median, faster for small medians along multiple
1103	    dimensions due to the high overhead of apply_along_axis
1104	
1105	    see nanmedian for parameter usage
1106	    """
1107	    a = np.ma.masked_array(a, np.isnan(a))
1108	    m = np.ma.median(a, axis=axis, overwrite_input=overwrite_input)
1109	    for i in range(np.count_nonzero(m.mask.ravel())):
1110	        warnings.warn("All-NaN slice encountered", RuntimeWarning,
1111	                      stacklevel=5)
1112	
1113	    fill_value = np.timedelta64("NaT") if m.dtype.kind == "m" else np.nan
1114	    if out is not None:
1115	        out[...] = m.filled(fill_value)
1116	        return out
1117	    return m.filled(fill_value)
1118

1125	@array_function_dispatch(_nanmedian_dispatcher)
1126	def nanmedian(a, axis=None, out=None, overwrite_input=False, keepdims=np._NoValue):
1127	    """
1128	    Compute the median along the specified axis, while ignoring NaNs.
1129	
1130	    Returns the median of the array elements.
1131	
1132	    Parameters
1133	    ----------
1134	    a : array_like
1135	        Input array or object that can be converted to an array.
1136	    axis : {int, sequence of int, None}, optional
1137	        Axis or axes along which the medians are computed. The default
1138	        is to compute the median along a flattened version of the array.
1139	        A sequence of axes is supported since version 1.9.0.
1140	    out : ndarray, optional
1141	        Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must
1142	        have the same shape and buffer length as the expected output,
1143	        but the type (of the output) will be cast if necessary.
1144	    overwrite_input : bool, optional
1145	       If True, then allow use of memory of input array `a` for
1146	       calculations. The input array will be modified by the call to
1147	       `median`. This will save memory when you do not need to preserve
1148	       the contents of the input array. Treat the input as undefined,
1149	       but it will probably be fully or partially sorted. Default is
1150	       False. If `overwrite_input` is ``True`` and `a` is not already an
1151	       `ndarray`, an error will be raised.
1152	    keepdims : bool, optional
1153	        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left
1154	        in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,
1155	        the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.
1156	
1157	        If this is anything but the default value it will be passed
1158	        through (in the special case of an empty array) to the
1159	        `mean` function of the underlying array.  If the array is
1160	        a sub-class and `mean` does not have the kwarg `keepdims` this
1161	        will raise a RuntimeError.
1162	
1163	    Returns
1164	    -------
1165	    median : ndarray
1166	        A new array holding the result. If the input contains integers,
1167	        the output data-type is ``float64``. Otherwise, the output data-type
1168	        is the same as that of the input. If `out` is specified, that array is
1169	        returned instead.
1170	
1171	    See Also
1172	    --------
1173	    mean, median, percentile
1174	
1175	    Notes
1176	    -----
1177	    Given a vector ``V`` of length ``N``, the median of ``V`` is the
1178	    middle value of a sorted copy of ``V``, ``V_sorted`` - i.e.,
1179	    ``V_sorted[(N-1)/2]``, when ``N`` is odd and the average of the two
1180	    middle values of ``V_sorted`` when ``N`` is even.
1181	
1182	    Examples
1183	    --------
1184	    >>> import numpy as np
1185	    >>> a = np.array([[10.0, 7, 4], [3, 2, 1]])
1186	    >>> a[0, 1] = np.nan
1187	    >>> a
1188	    array([[10., nan,  4.],
1189	           [ 3.,  2.,  1.]])
1190	    >>> np.median(a)
1191	    np.float64(nan)
1192	    >>> np.nanmedian(a)
1193	    3.0
1194	    >>> np.nanmedian(a, axis=0)
1195	    array([6.5, 2. , 2.5])
1196	    >>> np.median(a, axis=1)
1197	    array([nan,  2.])
1198	    >>> b = a.copy()
1199	    >>> np.nanmedian(b, axis=1, overwrite_input=True)
1200	    array([7.,  2.])
1201	    >>> assert not np.all(a==b)
1202	    >>> b = a.copy()
1203	    >>> np.nanmedian(b, axis=None, overwrite_input=True)
1204	    3.0
1205	    >>> assert not np.all(a==b)
1206	
1207	    """
1208	    a = np.asanyarray(a)
1209	    # apply_along_axis in _nanmedian doesn't handle empty arrays well,
1210	    # so deal them upfront
1211	    if a.size == 0:
1212	        return np.nanmean(a, axis, out=out, keepdims=keepdims)
1213	
1214	    return fnb._ureduce(a, func=_nanmedian, keepdims=keepdims,
1215	                        axis=axis, out=out,
1216	                        overwrite_input=overwrite_input)
1217

1225	@array_function_dispatch(_nanpercentile_dispatcher)
1226	def nanpercentile(
1227	        a,
1228	        q,

1231	        overwrite_input=False,
1232	        method="linear",
1233	        keepdims=np._NoValue,
1234	        *,
1235	        weights=None,

1237	    """
1238	    Compute the qth percentile of the data along the specified axis,
1239	    while ignoring nan values.
1240	
1241	    Returns the qth percentile(s) of the array elements.
1242	
1243	    Parameters
1244	    ----------
1245	    a : array_like
1246	        Input array or object that can be converted to an array, containing
1247	        nan values to be ignored.
1248	    q : array_like of float
1249	        Percentile or sequence of percentiles to compute, which must be
1250	        between 0 and 100 inclusive.
1251	    axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional
1252	        Axis or axes along which the percentiles are computed. The default
1253	        is to compute the percentile(s) along a flattened version of the
1254	        array.
1255	    out : ndarray, optional
1256	        Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must have
1257	        the same shape and buffer length as the expected output, but the
1258	        type (of the output) will be cast if necessary.
1259	    overwrite_input : bool, optional
1260	        If True, then allow the input array `a` to be modified by
1261	        intermediate calculations, to save memory. In this case, the
1262	        contents of the input `a` after this function completes is
1263	        undefined.
1264	    method : str, optional
1265	        This parameter specifies the method to use for estimating the
1266	        percentile.  There are many different methods, some unique to NumPy.
1267	        See the notes for explanation.  The options sorted by their R type
1268	        as summarized in the H&F paper [1]_ are:
1269	
1270	        1. 'inverted_cdf'
1271	        2. 'averaged_inverted_cdf'
1272	        3. 'closest_observation'
1273	        4. 'interpolated_inverted_cdf'
1274	        5. 'hazen'

1574	def _nanquantile_unchecked(
1575	        a,
1576	        q,
1577	        axis=None,
1578	        out=None,
1579	        overwrite_input=False,
1580	        method="linear",
1581	        keepdims=np._NoValue,
1582	        weights=None,
1583	        weak_q=False,
1584	):

1587	    # so deal them upfront

1589	        return np.nanmean(a, axis, out=out, keepdims=keepdims)
1590	    return fnb._ureduce(a,
1591	                        func=_nanquantile_ureduce_func,
1592	                        q=q,
1593	                        weights=weights,
1594	                        keepdims=keepdims,
1595	                        axis=axis,
1596	                        out=out,
1597	                        overwrite_input=overwrite_input,

1602	def _nanquantile_ureduce_func(
1603	        a: np.ndarray,
1604	        q: np.ndarray,
1605	        weights: np.ndarray,
1606	        axis: int | None = None,
1607	        out=None,
1608	        overwrite_input: bool = False,
1609	        method="linear",
1610	        weak_q=False,
1611	):
1612	    """
1613	    Private function that doesn't support extended axis or keepdims.
1614	    These methods are extended to this function using _ureduce
1615	    See nanpercentile for parameter usage
1616	    """
1617	    if axis is None or a.ndim == 1:
1618	        part = a.ravel()
1619	        wgt = None if weights is None else weights.ravel()
1620	        result = _nanquantile_1d(part, q, overwrite_input, method,
1621	                                 weights=wgt, weak_q=weak_q)
1622	    # Note that this code could try to fill in `out` right away
1623	    elif weights is None:
1624	        result = np.apply_along_axis(_nanquantile_1d, axis, a, q,
1625	                                     overwrite_input, method, weights, weak_q)
1626	        # apply_along_axis fills in collapsed axis with results.
1627	        # Move those axes to the beginning to match percentile's
1628	        # convention.
1629	        if q.ndim != 0:
1630	            from_ax = [axis + i for i in range(q.ndim)]
1631	            result = np.moveaxis(result, from_ax, list(range(q.ndim)))
1632	    else:
1633	        # We need to apply along axis over 2 arrays, a and weights.
1634	        # move operation axes to end for simplicity:
1635	        a = np.moveaxis(a, axis, -1)
1636	        if weights is not None:
1637	            weights = np.moveaxis(weights, axis, -1)
1638	        if out is not None:
1639	            result = out
1640	        else:
1641	            # weights are limited to `inverted_cdf` so the result dtype
1642	            # is known to be identical to that of `a` here:
1643	            result = np.empty_like(a, shape=q.shape + a.shape[:-1])
1644	
1645	        for ii in np.ndindex(a.shape[:-1]):
1646	            result[(...,) + ii] = _nanquantile_1d(
1647	                    a[ii], q, weights=weights[ii],
1648	                    overwrite_input=overwrite_input, method=method,
1649	                    weak_q=weak_q,
1650	            )
1651	        # This path dealt with `out` already...
1652	        return result
1653	
1654	    if out is not None:
1655	        out[...] = result
1656	    return result

1659	def _nanquantile_1d(
1660	    arr1d, q, overwrite_input=False, method="linear", weights=None,
1661	    weak_q=False,
1662	):
1663	    """
1664	    Private function for rank 1 arrays. Compute quantile ignoring NaNs.
1665	    See nanpercentile for parameter usage
1666	    """
1667	    # TODO: What to do when arr1d = [1, np.nan] and weights = [0, 1]?
1668	    arr1d, weights, overwrite_input = _remove_nan_1d(arr1d,
1669	        second_arr1d=weights, overwrite_input=overwrite_input)
1670	    if arr1d.size == 0:

1672	        return np.full(q.shape, np.nan, dtype=arr1d.dtype)[()]
1673	
1674	    return fnb._quantile_unchecked(
1675	        arr1d,
1676	        q,
1677	        overwrite_input=overwrite_input,
1678	        method=method,
1679	        weights=weights,
1680	        weak_q=weak_q,
1681	    )

1691	def nanvar(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, ddof=0, keepdims=np._NoValue,

1693	    """
1694	    Compute the variance along the specified axis, while ignoring NaNs.
1695	
1696	    Returns the variance of the array elements, a measure of the spread of
1697	    a distribution.  The variance is computed for the flattened array by
1698	    default, otherwise over the specified axis.
1699	
1700	    For all-NaN slices or slices with zero degrees of freedom, NaN is
1701	    returned and a `RuntimeWarning` is raised.
1702	
1703	    Parameters
1704	    ----------
1705	    a : array_like
1706	        Array containing numbers whose variance is desired.  If `a` is not an
1707	        array, a conversion is attempted.
1708	    axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional
1709	        Axis or axes along which the variance is computed.  The default is to compute
1710	        the variance of the flattened array.
1711	    dtype : data-type, optional
1712	        Type to use in computing the variance.  For arrays of integer type
1713	        the default is `float64`; for arrays of float types it is the same as
1714	        the array type.
1715	    out : ndarray, optional
1716	        Alternate output array in which to place the result.  It must have
1717	        the same shape as the expected output, but the type is cast if
1718	        necessary.
1719	    ddof : {int, float}, optional
1720	        "Delta Degrees of Freedom": the divisor used in the calculation is
1721	        ``N - ddof``, where ``N`` represents the number of non-NaN
1722	        elements. By default `ddof` is zero.
1723	    keepdims : bool, optional
1724	        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left
1725	        in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,
1726	        the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.
1727	    where : array_like of bool, optional
1728	        Elements to include in the variance. See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce` for
1729	        details.
1730	
1731	        .. versionadded:: 1.22.0
1732	
1733	    mean : array_like, optional
1734	        Provide the mean to prevent its recalculation. The mean should have
1735	        a shape as if it was calculated with ``keepdims=True``.
1736	        The axis for the calculation of the mean should be the same as used in
1737	        the call to this var function.
1738	
1739	        .. versionadded:: 2.0.0
1740	
1741	    correction : {int, float}, optional
1742	        Array API compatible name for the ``ddof`` parameter. Only one of them
1743	        can be provided at the same time.
1744	
1745	        .. versionadded:: 2.0.0
1746	
1747	    Returns
1748	    -------
1749	    variance : ndarray, see dtype parameter above
1750	        If `out` is None, return a new array containing the variance,
1751	        otherwise return a reference to the output array. If ddof is >= the
1752	        number of non-NaN elements in a slice or the slice contains only
1753	        NaNs, then the result for that slice is NaN.
1754

1756	    --------
1757	    std : Standard deviation
1758	    mean : Average
1759	    var : Variance while not ignoring NaNs
1760	    nanstd, nanmean

1763	    Notes

1765	    The variance is the average of the squared deviations from the mean,
1766	    i.e.,  ``var = mean(abs(x - x.mean())**2)``.
1767	
1768	    The mean is normally calculated as ``x.sum() / N``, where ``N = len(x)``.
1769	    If, however, `ddof` is specified, the divisor ``N - ddof`` is used
1770	    instead.  In standard statistical practice, ``ddof=1`` provides an
1771	    unbiased estimator of the variance of a hypothetical infinite
1772	    population.  ``ddof=0`` provides a maximum likelihood estimate of the
1773	    variance for normally distributed variables.

1776	    squaring, so that the result is always real and nonnegative.
1777	
1778	    For floating-point input, the variance is computed using the same
1779	    precision the input has.  Depending on the input data, this can cause
1780	    the results to be inaccurate, especially for `float32` (see example
1781	    below).  Specifying a higher-accuracy accumulator using the ``dtype``
1782	    keyword can alleviate this issue.

1785	    `sum` with the kwarg `keepdims`

1787	    Examples
1788	    --------
1789	    >>> import numpy as np
1790	    >>> a = np.array([[1, np.nan], [3, 4]])
1791	    >>> np.nanvar(a)
1792	    1.5555555555555554
1793	    >>> np.nanvar(a, axis=0)
1794	    array([1.,  0.])
1795	    >>> np.nanvar(a, axis=1)
1796	    array([0.,  0.25])  # may vary

1831	    else:
1832	        # we need to special case matrix for reverse compatibility
1833	        # in order for this to work, these sums need to be called with
1834	        # keepdims=True, however matrix now raises an error in this case, but
1835	        # the reason that it drops the keepdims kwarg is to force keepdims=True
1836	        # so this used to work by serendipity.
1837	        avg = np.sum(arr, axis=axis, dtype=dtype,
1838	                     keepdims=_keepdims, where=where)
1839	        avg = _divide_by_count(avg, cnt)
1840	
1841	    # Compute squared deviation from mean.
1842	    np.subtract(arr, avg, out=arr, casting='unsafe', where=where)
1843	    arr = _copyto(arr, 0, mask)
1844	    if issubclass(arr.dtype.type, np.complexfloating):
1845	        sqr = np.multiply(arr, arr.conj(), out=arr, where=where).real
1846	    else:
1847	        sqr = np.multiply(arr, arr, out=arr, where=where)
1848	
1849	    # Compute variance.
1850	    var = np.sum(sqr, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, keepdims=keepdims,
1851	                 where=where)
1852	
1853	    # Precaution against reduced object arrays
1854	    try:
1855	        var_ndim = var.ndim
1856	    except AttributeError:
1857	        var_ndim = np.ndim(var)
1858	    if var_ndim < cnt.ndim:
1859	        # Subclasses of ndarray may ignore keepdims, so check here.
1860	        cnt = cnt.squeeze(axis)
1861	    dof = cnt - ddof
1862	    var = _divide_by_count(var, dof)
1863	
1864	    isbad = (dof <= 0)
1865	    if np.any(isbad):
1866	        warnings.warn("Degrees of freedom <= 0 for slice.", RuntimeWarning,
1867	                      stacklevel=2)
1868	        # NaN, inf, or negative numbers are all possible bad
1869	        # values, so explicitly replace them with NaN.
1870	        var = _copyto(var, np.nan, isbad)
1871	    return var
1872	
1873	
1874	def _nanstd_dispatcher(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, ddof=None,
1875	                       keepdims=None, *, where=None, mean=None,
1876	                       correction=None):
1877	    return (a, out)
1878	
1879	
1880	@array_function_dispatch(_nanstd_dispatcher)
1881	def nanstd(a, axis=None, dtype=None, out=None, ddof=0, keepdims=np._NoValue,
1882	           *, where=np._NoValue, mean=np._NoValue, correction=np._NoValue):
1883	    """
1884	    Compute the standard deviation along the specified axis, while
1885	    ignoring NaNs.
1886	
1887	    Returns the standard deviation, a measure of the spread of a
1888	    distribution, of the non-NaN array elements. The standard deviation is
1889	    computed for the flattened array by default, otherwise over the
1890	    specified axis.
1891	
1892	    For all-NaN slices or slices with zero degrees of freedom, NaN is
1893	    returned and a `RuntimeWarning` is raised.
1894	
1895	    Parameters
1896	    ----------
1897	    a : array_like
1898	        Calculate the standard deviation of the non-NaN values.
1899	    axis : {int, tuple of int, None}, optional
1900	        Axis or axes along which the standard deviation is computed. The default is
1901	        to compute the standard deviation of the flattened array.
1902	    dtype : dtype, optional
1903	        Type to use in computing the standard deviation. For arrays of
1904	        integer type the default is float64, for arrays of float types it
1905	        is the same as the array type.
1906	    out : ndarray, optional
1907	        Alternative output array in which to place the result. It must have
1908	        the same shape as the expected output but the type (of the
1909	        calculated values) will be cast if necessary.
1910	    ddof : {int, float}, optional
1911	        Means Delta Degrees of Freedom.  The divisor used in calculations
1912	        is ``N - ddof``, where ``N`` represents the number of non-NaN
1913	        elements.  By default `ddof` is zero.
1914	
1915	    keepdims : bool, optional
1916	        If this is set to True, the axes which are reduced are left
1917	        in the result as dimensions with size one. With this option,
1918	        the result will broadcast correctly against the original `a`.
1919	
1920	        If this value is anything but the default it is passed through
1921	        as-is to the relevant functions of the sub-classes.  If these
1922	        functions do not have a `keepdims` kwarg, a RuntimeError will
1923	        be raised.
1924	    where : array_like of bool, optional
1925	        Elements to include in the standard deviation.
1926	        See `~numpy.ufunc.reduce` for details.
1927	
1928	        .. versionadded:: 1.22.0
1929	
1930	    mean : array_like, optional
1931	        Provide the mean to prevent its recalculation. The mean should have
1932	        a shape as if it was calculated with ``keepdims=True``.
1933	        The axis for the calculation of the mean should be the same as used in
1934	        the call to this std function.
1935	
1936	        .. versionadded:: 2.0.0
1937	
1938	    correction : {int, float}, optional
1939	        Array API compatible name for the ``ddof`` parameter. Only one of them
1940	        can be provided at the same time.
1941	
1942	        .. versionadded:: 2.0.0
1943	
1944	    Returns
1945	    -------
1946	    standard_deviation : ndarray, see dtype parameter above.
1947	        If `out` is None, return a new array containing the standard
1948	        deviation, otherwise return a reference to the output array. If
1949	        ddof is >= the number of non-NaN elements in a slice or the slice
1950	        contains only NaNs, then the result for that slice is NaN.
1951	
1952	    See Also
1953	    --------
1954	    var, mean, std
1955	    nanvar, nanmean
1956	    :ref:`ufuncs-output-type`
1957	
1958	    Notes
1959	    -----
1960	    The standard deviation is the square root of the average of the squared
1961	    deviations from the mean: ``std = sqrt(mean(abs(x - x.mean())**2))``.
1962	
1963	    The average squared deviation is normally calculated as
1964	    ``x.sum() / N``, where ``N = len(x)``.  If, however, `ddof` is
1965	    specified, the divisor ``N - ddof`` is used instead. In standard
1966	    statistical practice, ``ddof=1`` provides an unbiased estimator of the
1967	    variance of the infinite population. ``ddof=0`` provides a maximum
1968	    likelihood estimate of the variance for normally distributed variables.
1969	    The standard deviation computed in this function is the square root of
1970	    the estimated variance, so even with ``ddof=1``, it will not be an
1971	    unbiased estimate of the standard deviation per se.
1972	
1973	    Note that, for complex numbers, `std` takes the absolute value before
1974	    squaring, so that the result is always real and nonnegative.
1975	
1976	    For floating-point input, the *std* is computed using the same
1977	    precision the input has. Depending on the input data, this can cause
1978	    the results to be inaccurate, especially for float32 (see example
1979	    below).  Specifying a higher-accuracy accumulator using the `dtype`
1980	    keyword can alleviate this issue.
1981	
1982	    Examples
1983	    --------
1984	    >>> import numpy as np
1985	    >>> a = np.array([[1, np.nan], [3, 4]])
1986	    >>> np.nanstd(a)
1987	    1.247219128924647
1988	    >>> np.nanstd(a, axis=0)
1989	    array([1., 0.])
1990	    >>> np.nanstd(a, axis=1)
1991	    array([0.,  0.5]) # may vary
1992	
1993	    """
1994	    var = nanvar(a, axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, ddof=ddof,
1995	                 keepdims=keepdims, where=where, mean=mean,
1996	                 correction=correction)
1997	    if isinstance(var, np.ndarray):
1998	        std = np.sqrt(var, out=var)
1999	    elif hasattr(var, 'dtype'):
2000	        std = var.dtype.type(np.sqrt(var))
2001	    else:
2002	        std = np.sqrt(var)
2003	    return std
2004
